name: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)
supplementation_notes: Take with food to improve absorption. Alcohol depletes thiamine.
drug_nutrient_interactions: Diuretics reduce thiamine absorption;Alcohol impairs thiamine metabolism

name: Vitamin B3 (Niacin)
supplementation_notes: Can cause flushing at high doses. Take with food to reduce GI upset.

name: Folate (Vitamin B9)
deficiency_symptoms: fatigue;weakness;memory problems;pale skin;mouth sores;nervous system issues
supplementation_notes: Essential during pregnancy. Plant sources less bioavailable than supplements.
drug_nutrient_interactions: Metformin reduces folate absorption;PPIs reduce absorption;Antibiotics affect gut bacteria

name: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
supplementation_notes: Can be depleted by stress and antibiotics.

name: Zinc
deficiency_symptoms: fatigue;hair loss;poor wound healing;weak immunity;rashes;loss of taste
supplementation_notes: Better absorbed with protein. Do not exceed 40 mg daily from supplements.
drug_nutrient_interactions: Corticosteroids increase loss;Diuretics increase excretion;PPIs reduce absorption

name: Copper
supplementation_notes: Excess zinc interferes with copper absorption. Balance is important.
absorption_factors: Zinc ratio (too much zinc impairs);acidic stomach (enhance)

name: Selenium
supplementation_notes: Works synergistically with vitamin E. Excess can be toxic.

name: Vitamin C
supplementation_notes: Take daily as body doesn't store it. Enhances iron absorption.

name: Vitamin E
supplementation_notes: Fat-soluble vitamin. Take with fat-containing meal. Antioxidant functions.
drug_nutrient_interactions: Warfarin may interact;Bile acid sequestrants reduce absorption

name: Chromium
supplementation_notes: Helps regulate blood sugar. Often low in Western diets.
